rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is explicit_self. The source explicitly targets peatland restoration: “For peatland restoration… the area of degraded peatland being restored should reach 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050… under LCCP… 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050.” Peatland restoration is a Nature-Based Solutions action consistent with peat emissions avoidance/carbon sequestration; the gate failure appears due to missing outcome token rather than non-climate content.
policy_statement: Indonesia sets peatland restoration targets: CPOS 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050; LCCP 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050.
mechanism_description: It sets targets to restore degraded peatlands, which helps re-wet peat soils and reduces emissions from peat degradation and fires while supporting carbon storage.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration (peatland re-wetting) → ↓ peat oxidation + ↓ peat fire risk → ↓ CO₂ emissions + ↑ long-term carbon storage → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Land and water management shifts from drained/degraded peatlands to restored, re-wetted peat ecosystems (e.g., blocking drainage canals, raising water tables, re-vegetation, restricting conversion).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Higher water tables create anaerobic conditions that greatly reduce peat decomposition/oxidation; wetter conditions also reduce ignition probability and fire spread, cutting combustion emissions; over time, peat-forming vegetation can rebuild carbon stocks.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net GHG emissions (primarily CO₂; sometimes also reduced fire-related CH₄/CO/black carbon) and increased carbon sequestration/storage in peat soils and associated biomass.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions (especially CO₂ from peat oxidation and fires) by re-wetting and restoring peatlands, avoiding emissions and enabling long-term carbon sequestration.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Peatland restoration changes ecosystem condition (rewetting) which reduces peat oxidation and fire, delivering large avoided CO₂ emissions and supporting long-term carbon storage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can also improve water regulation and reduce smoke/heat impacts (adaptation/co-benefits), the most material climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions avoidance and sequestration via ecosystem carbon dynamics—hallmark of Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some peat actions could increase short-term CH₄ from re-wetting or be framed as fire/haze risk management; however, the dominant, intended climate-relevant effect remains net GHG reduction through avoided peat loss and fires.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that restores/re-wets peatlands to stop degradation necessarily operates through ecosystem restoration affecting peat carbon processes, so the primary category remains Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Specific subtypes (e.g., “carbon sequestration” vs “emissions avoidance,” “fire risk reduction,” or “water regulation”) depend on policy design and quantified evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Re-wetting can increase CH₄ emissions in some contexts; classification still follows the dominant net climate effect (typically large CO₂/fire-emissions avoidance). If a policy is purely about agricultural productivity or haze control without restoration/rewetting as the core mechanism, it may not belong to this mechanism class.
key_indicators: “target”; “should reach 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050”; “the target will be increased to 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050”; “peatland restoration”; “area of degraded peatland being restored”
co_benefits: Reduced peat fire smoke/air pollution and related health impacts; biodiversity/habitat benefits; improved water regulation and local livelihoods (not explicitly stated in the text)
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be interpreted as a broader planning commitment, but the verbatim text contains only quantified area goals and timelines (no plan/process elements or implementation instruments), so it is classified as target-setting rather than planning-strategy.
